coredata.dc:title: Effect of spark plasma sintering temperature on thermoelectric properties of (Ti,Zr,Hf)NiSn half-Heusler compounds
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kurosaki, Ken
Maekawa, Takuji
Muta, Hiroaki
Yamanaka, Shinsuke
coredata.dc:description: The effect of spark plasma sintering (SPS) temperature on thermoelectric properties of (Ti,Zr,Hf)NiSn half-Heusler compounds were studied. Four kinds of samples were prepared: an arc-melted sample, a sample sintered at 973K by SPS (SPS-973), a sample sintered at 1173K by SPS (SPS-1173), and a sample sintered at 1373K by SPS (SPS-1373). The power factor of SPS-1173 is several % lower than that of the arc-melted sample, but the thermal conductivity is reduced to 70% of that of the arc-melted sample. As a result, the dimensionless figure of merit ZT of SPS-1173 is improved and it reaches 0.43 at 760K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Half-Heusler
Thermoelectric
Electrical transport
Thermal conductivity
Spark plasma sintering

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples, together with literature data of ZrNiSn [13].

Microstructure and element distribution of the arc-melted sample: The grain matrix composed of half-Heusler compound is represented by +1, and the grain boundary composed of Ti–Sn based alloy is represented by +2.
Microstructure and element distribution of the spark plasma sintered sample: The SPS temperature is 1173K. The Ti-rich and Ti-poor half-Heusler phases are represented by +1 and +2, respectively.
Temperature dependence of the electrical properties of arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples: (a) electrical resistivity; (b) Seebeck coefficient; (c) power factor.
Temperature dependence of the thermal conductivity of arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples.
Temperature dependence of the ZT of arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples.
Characteristics of arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples of (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn

article.ce:title: Effect of spark plasma sintering temperature on thermoelectric properties of (Ti,Zr,Hf)NiSn half-Heusler compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Engineering, Graduate School of Engineering, Osaka University, Yamadaoka 2-1, Suita, Osaka 565-0871, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of spark plasma sintering (SPS) temperature on thermoelectric properties of (Ti,Zr,Hf)NiSn half-Heusler compounds were studied. Four kinds of samples were prepared: an arc-melted sample, a sample sintered at 973K by SPS (SPS-973), a sample sintered at 1173K by SPS (SPS-1173), and a sample sintered at 1373K by SPS (SPS-1373). The power factor of SPS-1173 is several % lower than that of the arc-melted sample, but the thermal conductivity is reduced to 70% of that of the arc-melted sample. As a result, the dimensionless figure of merit ZT of SPS-1173 is improved and it reaches 0.43 at 760K.

ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of a material for thermoelectric applications is determined by the dimensionless figure of merit ZT [1], where T is the absolute temperature and Z =(S 2 σ)/κ (S is the Seebeck coefficient, σ the electrical conductivity, and κ the thermal conductivity). The electrical properties are determined by the power factor, defined here as S 2 σ or S 2/ρ, where ρ is the electrical resistivity.

We have taken particular note of 18 electron half-Heusler compounds, which were discovered by Jeischko [2], as new thermoelectric materials because of their semiconductor like band structure [3–11]. Hohl et al. [6] studied the effect of dopant on the thermoelectric properties of ZrNiSn type half-Heusler compounds, and reported that hafnium and/or titanium substitution for zirconium leads to a higher thermoelectric figure of merit. In our previous study [12], we have studied the thermoelectric properties of titanium based half-Heusler compounds (Zr x Hf1−x )0.7Ti0.3NiSn, and found that (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn has the highest power factor but also has the highest thermal conductivity.

In the present study, in order to reduce the thermal conductivity of (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn, high-density polycrystalline sintered samples are prepared by a powder metallurgical method using spark plasma sintering (SPS). The thermoelectric properties of sintered samples are measured and compared with that of arc-melted sample.

(Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn was prepared by arc melting from the constituent elements followed by annealing at 1000K for 2 days under vacuum in sealed quartz ampoules. The thus obtained ingot were crushed and passed through a sieve with an aperture size of 75μm. The granulated powder was treated by SPS at three temperatures 973, 1173, and 1373K. The samples were designated as SPS-973, SPS-1173, and SPS-1373, respectively. The crystal structure was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) at room temperature using Cu Kα radiation. The microstructure and chemical composition were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). The density was determined from the measured weight and dimensions. In the temperature range between room temperature and about 1000K, the electrical resistivity and Seebeck coefficient were measured simultaneously using a ULVAC ZEM-1 apparatus in helium atmosphere. The Seebeck coefficient was measured in a temperature gradient of about 10K. The thermal diffusivity was measured by a laser flash method using a ULVAC TC-7000 apparatus from room temperature to about 1000K in vacuum. The heat capacity was evaluated from those of the constituent elements using Kopp's law. The thermal conductivity κ was evaluated from the thermal diffusivity, heat capacity, and measured density.

The XRD patterns of arc-melted and sintered samples of (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn showed that the half-Heusler compound is obtained in the present study. The lattice parameter evaluated from the XRD pattern was 0.6090nm, which is not changed by the SPS process. The bulk density of arc-melted sample is about 100% of the theoretical density. On the other hand, for the SPS samples, the bulk densities of SPS-973, SPS-1173, and SPS-1373 are about 70, 90, and 95% of the theoretical density, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the arc-melted and spark plasma sintered samples, together with literature data of ZrNiSn [13]. In the XRD patterns of SPS-1173 and SPS-1373, other peaks are found at the right shoulder of the main peaks. It can be seen that the peak intensity increases with increasing SPS temperature.

The microstructure and element distribution of the arc-melted sample are shown in Fig. 2 . The grain and grain boundary structure are observed in the SEM image. From the EDX analysis, the grain is composed of the half-Heusler compound with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn, and the grain boundary is composed of Ti–Sn based alloy. Since the amount of Ti–Sn based alloy is very small, the peaks caused by the alloy are not observed in the XRD pattern.

The microstructure and element distribution of spark plasma sintered sample are shown in Fig. 3 . The SPS temperature is 1173K. The microstructure and element distributions are very different from those of the arc-melted sample. The SPS-1173 has no grain boundary but has some pores. From the EDX analysis, it is found that there are two phases: the first phase is a Ti-poor phase with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn, and the second phase is a Ti-rich phase with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.6Ti0.4NiSn. According to Shannon's ionic radii [14], the ionic radius of tetravalent titanium with six-coordination is smaller than those of zirconium and hafnium (VITi4+ =0.0605nm, VIZr4+ =0.071nm, VIHf4+ =0.072nm). As a result, the lattice parameter of the Ti-rich half-Heusler compound (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.6Ti0.4NiSn is smaller than that of the Ti-poor half-Heusler compound (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn. Therefore, the other peaks observed at the right shoulder of the main peaks in Fig. 1 are those of the Ti-rich half-Heusler compound (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.6Ti0.4NiSn. The same results are obtained for SPS-1373. It seems that the second phase (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.6Ti0.4NiSn is formed in addition to the matrix phase of (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn by SPS at high temperatures. The characteristics of the arc-melted and SPS samples are summarized in Table 1 .

The temperature dependences of the electrical properties of the arc-melted and SPS samples are shown in Fig. 4 . The electrical resistivities of all samples decrease with increasing temperature, showing semiconducting characteristics. The electrical resistivity of SPS-973 is three times higher than that of the arc-melted sample. The high resistivity of SPS-973 is caused by the low density of the sample. The values of electrical resistivities of SPS-1173 and SPS-1373 are almost the same, and they are about 15% lower than that of the arc-melted sample at high temperatures.

The Seebeck coefficients are negative in the whole temperature range, showing that the majority of charge carriers are electrons. The absolute values of the Seebeck coefficients of SPS-1173 and SPS-1373 are 10% lower than that of the arc-melted sample in the whole temperature range. The power factor, given as S 2/ρ, of SPS-973 is 75% lower than that of the arc-melted sample. On the other hand, the power factors of SPS-1173 and SPS-1373 are just a few % lower than that of the arc-melted sample.

The temperature dependence of the thermal conductivities κ of the arc-melted and sintered samples is shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen in this figure, the thermal conductivities increase gradually with increasing temperature. The thermal conductivities of SPS samples are lower than that of the arc-melted sample. The values at 330K are 1.4Wm−1 K−1 for SPS-973, 2.9Wm−1 K−1 for SPS-1173, 3.2Wm−1 K−1 for SPS-1373, and 3.9Wm−1 K−1 for the arc-melted sample. Low temperature SPS leads to low densification and low thermal conductivity.

The dimensionless figures of merit ZT of the arc-melted and SPS samples are evaluated by using the data of the electrical resistivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity. The temperature dependence of ZT is shown in Fig. 6 . The ZT of all samples increase with increasing temperature, and reach a maximum value at around 750K. The values of ZT of SPS-1173 and SPS-1373 are almost the same. The ZT of both SPS-1173 and SPS-1373 are larger than that of the arc-melted sample, due to an effective reduction of the thermal conductivity without degradation of the electrical performance. The maximum value of ZT is obtained as 0.43 at 760K for SPS-1173.

The effect of SPS temperature on the thermoelectric properties of titanium based half-Heusler compounds (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.7Ti0.3NiSn, was studied. In the arc-melted sample (non-SPS sample), the grain and grain boundary structure are observed, in which the grain is composed of the half-Heusler compound with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn, and the grain boundary is composed of a Ti–Sn based alloy. In the SPS samples (SPS-1173 and SPS-1373), there are two phases: the first phase is a Ti-poor phase with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.9Ti0.1NiSn, and the second phase is a Ti-rich phase with the composition (Zr0.6Hf0.4)0.6Ti0.4NiSn. The power factor of SPS-1173 is about 5% lower than that of the arc-melted sample, while the thermal conductivity is reduced to about 70% of that of the arc-melted sample. It can be achieved that the thermal conductivity is efficiently reduced without degradation of the electrical performance by the SPS process. The ZT of SPS-1173 reaches 0.43 at 760K, which is 30% higher than that of the arc-melted sample.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. K. Niihara, Prof. T. Sekino, Dr. T. Kusunose, and Dr. T. Nakayama (The Institute of Science and Industrial Research, Osaka University) for the sample preparation using SPS apparatus.
